body:
SURFACED while drafting §28 (2026-07-21), when Myk asked whether §28's no-cache rule commits Loam to gradual performance degradation. It does not - but the answer turned up a degradation that ALREADY EXISTS and predates §28 entirely.

THE FINDING. `lawfulSnapshot` (src/gateway/registration.ts) is a FULL SCAN of the reactor snapshot, materialized into a new DeltaSet:

    return DeltaSet.from([...reactor.snapshot()].filter((d) => d.claims.author === operator));

And `readTrustPolicy` (src/gateway/trust.ts) calls it TWICE per invocation - once inside `lawfulNegated` (which also builds a Set of every lawful delta id) and once again to iterate looking for the handful of deltas that point at `loam:trust`. So resolving the trust policy is O(N) with two full passes, where N is EVERY DELTA IN THE STORE, and it runs per request (trust.ts and budget.ts both advertise 're-resolved from the live deltas each request' as a feature - and it IS one; the freshness is correct, the cost is not).

The ground is grow-only. N only ever increases. So the trust path gets monotonically slower for the life of the store, to find a declaration set that is typically one delta. The same shape almost certainly affects the other constitutional readers built on `lawfulSnapshot`/`lawfulNegated` - `readRegistrations`, `readBindingDefinitions`, `grantHeld`, budget resolution - so measure before assuming this is only trust.

WHY IT MATTERS NOW. §28 makes the trusted set a walk over the CONTAINER TREE rather than a single root lookup, so this per-request O(N) gets multiplied by tree depth. Depth is small (root -> tenant -> maybe a quarantine), so the multiplier is ~3 rather than alarming - but multiplying a cost that already grows without bound is the wrong direction, and §28 would take the blame for a problem it merely made visible.

THE FIX IS AN INDEX, NOT A CACHE - and the distinction is the whole ticket. §28 prohibits caching the FLATTENED TRUSTED SET, because that is a correctness-critical derived value which, if stored, goes stale silently and produces a well-formed wrong answer (a revoked author still binding). That prohibition says nothing about making the COMPUTATION fast. An index by (target entity, context) is a different object: maintained on ingest rather than stored by a caller who might forget to refresh, and it changes the cost of finding the declarations without changing what is computed from them. Materializations are already exactly this kind of structure; this is asking for the same discipline one layer down.

BUILD (recommendation, not prescribed): give the reactor-side lookup a by-entity/by-context path so a constitutional reader asks for 'deltas pointing at loam:trust' instead of scanning and filtering. If rhizomatic already exposes such a path, use it and this is a small refactor; if it does not, this may be a substrate conversation (rhizomatic is FROZEN - file, do not fork). Establish which BEFORE designing, and record the answer in the ticket.

RAILS - and the first one is the point of the ticket:
- A LATENCY rail with a growing store: resolve the trust policy against a store of N deltas and again at 10N, where the number of trust declarations is unchanged. The cost must not grow proportionally to N. Assert the relationship, not an absolute number, so the rail survives a fast machine.
- Freshness is PRESERVED: every existing guarantee holds unchanged - a roster edit binds on the next request, and §7's 'revoking a grant un-binds its author's strikes on the very next read' still holds. This ticket must not buy speed with staleness; that trade is exactly what §28 forbids.
- The other constitutional readers keep their current semantics exactly - `test/gateway/` should stay green with no assertion softened.

NOT IN SCOPE: any cache of a resolved policy or trusted set. If profiling later shows an index is insufficient, that is a design change needing its own invalidation story (§28.6 says so), not a local optimization someone adds in this ticket.
title: Constitutional reads scan the whole store, every request - index them before the tree multiplies it
